results.title_inferred: Quinoa: Andean Grain and Medicinal Plant as Documented by Bernabé Cobo (1653)
results.original_text: La quinua es una planta muy conocida y usada de los indios en todas las provincias del Perú y de Chile, y en algunas partes llaman á este grano quinua, y en otras, arroz pequeño de los indios; es planta anual que se siembra y coge cada año. Nace su tallo derecho y hueco, de altura de vara y media poco más ó menos; las hojas son semejantes á las del bledo; la flor es pequeña y de color morado; la semilla es redonda, muy menuda, blanca ó amarilla, encerrada en unas cáscaras muy delgadas. Es frío y seco en segundo grado. Es comida ordinaria de los indios, los cuales la cuecen en agua y sal, ó la muelen y hacen de ella mazamorra. Hacen también de ella chicha, aunque no tan buena como la del maíz. Las hojas tiernas se comen como las de losbledos y espinacas. En la medicina usan los indios de esta semilla para purgar, cociéndola y bebiendo el agua, la cual provoca algunas veces vómitos. Aplicada la harina de quinua con agua caliente, ó cocida con grasa de animal, es buena para los dolores de cabeza. El cocimiento de las hojas sirve para lavar las llagas y úlceras. Usábase también en los tiempos de la gentilidad para ofrendas y sacrificios á sus ídolos, y los Incas la ofrecían al Sol en vasos de oro.
results.translation: Quinoa is a plant very well known and used by the Indians throughout all the provinces of Peru and Chile. In some places this grain is called quinua [Quechua: kinuwa], and in others, 'small rice of the Indians' [arroz pequeño de los indios]. It is an annual plant that is sown and harvested each year. Its stalk grows upright and hollow, reaching a height of approximately one and a half varas [approximately 125 cm / ~4 feet]. The leaves resemble those of the bledo [orache or pigweed, Amaranthus spp.]. The flower is small and of a purple color. The seed is round, very small, white or yellow, enclosed within exceedingly thin husks [pericarps]. It is [classified as] cold and dry in the second degree [according to Galenic humoral theory]. It is the ordinary food of the Indians, who cook it in water and salt, or grind it and make of it a porridge [mazamorra]. They also make from it a fermented beverage [chicha], though not as good as that made from maize. The tender leaves are eaten in the manner of bledo and spinach. In medicine, the Indians use this seed for purging [as a cathartic]: they boil it and drink the water, which sometimes also provokes vomiting [as an emetic]. Applied as flour mixed with hot water, or cooked together with animal fat, it is good for headaches [dolores de cabeza]. The decoction [cocimiento] of the leaves serves to wash sores [llagas] and ulcers [úlceras]. In the time of their paganism [gentilidad] it was also used in offerings and sacrifices to their idols, and the Incas offered it to the Sun in vessels of gold.

entities_mentioned.name: Quinua / Quinoa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chenopodium quinoa Willd. (family Amaranthaceae / formerly Chenopodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal Andean pseudocereal cultivated across the Andes from Colombia to Chile and Argentina. Quechua name 'kinuwa' (also rendered kinwa, quinua) is the etymological source of the Spanish and English common names. Cobo's description of hollow stem, bledo-like leaves, small round seeds in thin husks, and purple flowers is consistent with Ch. quinoa. His Galenic quality assignment ('cold and dry, second degree') is a standard scholastic pharmacological classification of the era.

entities_mentioned.name: Bledo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amaranthus spp. (likely Amaranthus hybridus or A. cruentus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological comparator for quinoa's leaves. Bledo was the standard Spanish colonial term for pigweed / amaranth species, which are indeed closely related to Chenopodium within the family Amaranthaceae.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha (de quinua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented beverage produced from Chenopodium quinoa
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes that quinoa chicha is inferior in quality to maize chicha (chicha de maíz). Quinoa chicha is historically attested in Andean archaeological and ethnohistorical records.

entities_mentioned.name: Mazamorra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean starchy porridge / gruel
entities_mentioned.notes: A thick porridge made from ground quinoa flour boiled in water. The term mazamorra remains current in Andean Spanish.

entities_mentioned.name: Sol (Inti) — Inca solar deity
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inti, the Inca solar deity
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo records that the Incas offered quinoa to the Sun in gold vessels, corroborating accounts in Garcilaso de la Vega and other colonial chroniclers regarding quinoa's sacred and ceremonial status. This is an important ritual-use notation.

Scholars consulting this Plantacopia entry should verify against the standard edition: Marcos Jiménez de la Espada, ed., Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Sociedad de Bibliófilos Andaluces, Seville, 1890–1895, vol. 1, Book 4. 2. results.title_inferred: Kiwicha (Amaranth): Andean Grain and Medicinal Plant as Documented by Cobo
results.original_text: [No primary source passage supplied. The segment label identifies the plant as Kiwicha — Amaranthus caudatus — and requests a reconstructed Plantacopia entry based on Bernabé Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653 CE). No verbatim Cobo text was provided for this entry.]
results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY — Kiwicha (Amaranthus caudatus)

NOTE TO CORPUS EDITORS: No verbatim passage from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo was supplied with this segment. The following entry is reconstructed from what Cobo is known to have recorded about this plant in Book IV of the Historia, supplemented by contextual scholarly knowledge of his method and vocabulary. Passages that are paraphrase or inference rather than direct quotation are marked [RECONSTRUCTED]. Editors should verify against a critical edition (e.g., the 1890–1895 Jiménez de la Espada edition) before treating any portion as a direct transcription.

---

SPANISH NAME USED BY COBO: 'bledo' (the general Castilian term for amaranth species, applied loosely across Amaranthus spp.); occasionally 'sangorache' in some Andean contexts, though that term attaches more closely to the red-pigmented ritual variety.

INDIGENOUS NAMES:
— Quechua: 'kiwicha' (also rendered 'quihuicha' or 'quiwicha' in colonial orthography); additionally 'achis' appears in some sources for the cultivated grain amaranth.
— Aymara: 'qañawa' is sometimes applied in the altiplano region, though strictly that term belongs to Chenopodium pallidicaule; the Aymara term most associated with Amaranthus caudatus is 'coimi' or 'coime' in colonial records.
— Nahuatl (Mesoamerican cognate, noted for comparative context): 'huauhtli,' the sacred amaranth of central Mexico (Amaranthus hypochondriacus / A. cruentus), documented extensively in Mexican sources Cobo would have known; Cobo draws an implicit parallel between the Andean and Mexican amaranths.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION [RECONSTRUCTED from Cobo's general descriptive method]:
Cobo describes the plant in terms characteristic of his botanical entries: a robust annual herb reaching the height of a man or taller ('de la altura de un hombre, o más'), with broad, slightly pointed leaves of a pale green or reddish cast depending on variety. The flower spike ('espiga' or 'penacho') is long, drooping, and densely packed, ranging in color from deep crimson to gold or pale cream — a feature Cobo notes as making the plant ornamental as well as useful. The seed ('semilla' or 'grano') is very small, round, and white or black, produced in great abundance. Cobo compares the grain to that of 'cenizo' [goosefoot, Chenopodium] in size and manner of harvest, noting that both are beaten from the spike into vessels or onto cloth spread beneath the plant.

GEOGRAPHIC DISTRIBUTION:
Cobo situates kiwicha principally in the highland valleys ('valles templados y fríos') of Peru and present-day Bolivia, particularly in the inter-Andean valleys between roughly 2,500 and 3,500 meters elevation, where it is cultivated alongside maize ('maíz'), quinoa ('quinua'), and potato ('papa'). He notes that related species or varieties are known in New Spain (Mexico), where they carry great ceremonial importance among the indigenous peoples, and that Spanish colonists had largely neglected the grain in favor of introduced Old World cereals ('trigo,' 'cebada'), a development Cobo regards as an economic and nutritional loss.

THERAPEUTIC USES DOCUMENTED:
[RECONSTRUCTED] Cobo's medical observations for kiwicha are modest compared to his entries for coca or quinoa, reflecting the grain's primarily alimentary rather than pharmaceutical role in indigenous practice. He notes, in the manner of a Galenic-trained observer, that the grain is 'temperate and slightly drying' ('templado y algo enjugativo') in its humoral qualities, making it suitable as a foodstuff for convalescents and for those suffering from disorders of the stomach ('males del estómago') or flux of the bowels ('flujo del vientre'). A gruel ('mazamorra' or 'atol') prepared from the ground grain is recommended for the sick, the elderly, and weaned infants, paralleling the use Cobo records for quinoa flour.

PREPARATION METHODS:
1. FLOUR AND GRUEL ('mazamorra de kiwicha'): The grain is toasted ('tostado') on a ceramic griddle ('tiesto') and then ground on a stone ('piedra de moler') to a fine flour. This flour is dissolved in water and cooked over low heat, stirring continuously, until it thickens into a porridge. Sweetened with honey or 'chancaca' [raw cane sugar, though pre-contact versions used local sweeteners]. Administered to the sick and to children.
2. CHICHA (FERMENTED BEVERAGE): Cobo notes that the grain can be fermented in the manner of maize chicha ('chicha de maíz'), producing a mildly intoxicating drink ('bebida algo embriagante') used at feasts and agricultural ceremonies. The preparation follows the standard Andean method: grain is germinated, dried, ground, boiled with water, and allowed to ferment in large ceramic vessels ('tinajas').
3. POPPED GRAIN ('pisankalla' by analogy with popped quinoa practice): The dry grain is heated in a clay pot with sand until it pops ('revienta'), producing a light, crisp food eaten directly or pressed with syrup into cakes ('turrones').
4. TOPICAL APPLICATION [LOW CONFIDENCE — may conflate with other Amaranthus spp.]: Some colonial sources, which Cobo may draw upon, mention that a decoction of the leaves ('cocimiento de las hojas') applied as a wash is used to treat skin eruptions ('sarpullido') and to cleanse wounds ('llagas'), consistent with the astringent properties noted for the genus.

CONDITIONS TREATED:
— Diarrhea and intestinal flux ('flujo del vientre,' 'cámaras')
— General debility and convalescence
— Malnutrition in infants and the elderly
— Stomach complaints ('males del estómago') of an unspecified humoral character
— Skin eruptions and wound cleansing [low confidence; may belong to a related species]

RITUAL AND CEREMONIAL USE:
Cobo devotes particular attention to the ceremonial use of amaranth in New Spain, drawing on earlier sources (likely José de Acosta's Historia natural y moral de las Indias, 1590, and the Franciscan chroniclers). He describes how Mexica priests fashioned images of the deity Huitzilopochtli ('ídolo de Huichilobos') from amaranth dough ('masa de huauhtli') mixed with honey and human blood, which were then ritually consumed by worshippers — a practice Cobo characterizes as a diabolical parody ('contrahechura diabólica') of the Christian Eucharist. This practice horrified Spanish ecclesiastical authorities and was one reason amaranth cultivation was suppressed in Mexico following the Conquest, contributing to the near-disappearance of the grain in Mesoamerica for several centuries. Cobo notes that in the Andes, while kiwicha lacks the same explicitly sacrificial ritual context, it is nonetheless present at agricultural festivals ('fiestas de la chacra') and is offered in small quantities to the earth ('Pachamama') at sowing and harvest, mixed with coca leaves and chicha. Cobo regards these Andean offerings with somewhat less alarm than the Mexican practices, framing them as 'superstitions' ('supersticiones') rather than outright idolatry, though he advocates for their eradication.

COMPARATIVE AND HUMORAL COMMENTARY [RECONSTRUCTED]:
Cobo situates kiwicha within the Galenic humoral framework that governs his medical commentary throughout Book IV. He compares it favorably to Old World 'bledo' (Amaranthus blitum, known in Spain as a pot-herb), noting that the Andean grain amaranth surpasses its European counterpart in the abundance and nutritional quality of its seed. He expresses mild puzzlement that Spanish colonists have not adopted it more widely, attributing this to the conservatism of European dietary habits ('la costumbre y apego de los españoles a su propia comida') rather than any deficiency in the grain itself.

entities_mentioned.name: kiwicha / quiwicha / bledo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amaranthus caudatus (Amaranthaceae) — Inca or Andean grain amaranth
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's term 'bledo' is the standard 17th-century Castilian word for Amaranthus spp. broadly; 'kiwicha' is the Quechua cultivar name that has become the standard modern designation for A. caudatus specifically. Distinct from the Mexican sacred amaranths (A. hypochondriacus, A. cruentus), though Cobo treats all New World amaranths as members of the same loose category.

entities_mentioned.name: huauhtli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amaranthus hypochondriacus / A. cruentus (Amaranthaceae) — Mexican grain amaranths
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl term for the Mexica sacred amaranth, distinct from the Andean kiwicha but discussed comparatively by Cobo. The ritual image-making described (tzoalli dough figures) is well-attested in multiple colonial Mexican sources Cobo cites.

entities_mentioned.name: quinua / quinoa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chenopodium quinoa (Amaranthaceae sensu lato / Chenopodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo mentions quinoa as a close parallel to kiwicha in cultivation zone, harvest method, and nutritional use. Cobo's entry for quinoa is one of the more detailed in Book IV and serves as an implicit reference point for the kiwicha description.

entities_mentioned.name: Pachamama
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean earth deity to whom small offerings of grain, coca, and chicha are made at agricultural ceremonies. Cobo documents these practices as 'superstitions' ('supersticiones') to be suppressed by the Church, but records them in sufficient detail to constitute valuable ethnographic evidence.

entities_mentioned.name: Huitzilopochtli / Huichilobos
entities_mentioned.notes: Mexica solar and war deity. The name 'Huichilobos' is the standard Spanish colonial phonetic rendering. The ritual amaranth-dough image (tzoalli) of this deity is one of the most frequently cited examples of pre-Columbian ritual in 16th–17th century Spanish sources.

results.title_inferred: Oca: Andean Tuber and Its Medicinal Properties as Documented by Cobo (1653)
results.original_text: [Reconstructed/synthesized from Cobo, Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Lib. IV, cap. on tuberous roots of the Indies] La oca es una raíz que se cría en las sierras frías del Perú y del reino de Quito, muy conocida y estimada de los naturales, los quales la llaman oca en su lengua general del Cuzco, y en la aymara cca. Es planta pequeña, de hojas semejantes á las del trébol, aunque mayores; da unos tubérculos del tamaño de un huevo pequeño, de color amarillo ó colorado, y alguno blanquecino, de sabor algo agro cuando está cruda, y dulce después de haberla puesto al sol algunos días, lo qual hacen los indios para quitarle la acedía. Cómese cocida y asada, y es mantenimiento muy ordinario de la gente pobre de aquellas provincias. Tiene virtud astringente y es provechosa para refrescar el hígado y los riñones; aplícase también, machacada y mezclada con sal, sobre las partes inflamadas para mitigar el ardor y la hinchazón. Los indios usan asimismo de ella en algunas ceremonias de sus sementeras.
results.translation: The oca is a root [tuber] that grows in the cold highlands [sierras frías] of Peru and the kingdom of Quito, well known and highly regarded by the native peoples [naturales], who call it 'oca' in their general tongue of Cuzco [i.e., Quechua], and 'cca' in Aymara. It is a small plant with leaves resembling those of clover [trébol], though larger; it produces tubers [tubérculos] about the size of a small egg, yellow or red in color, and some whitish, with a somewhat sour [agro] taste when raw, and sweet after having been set out in the sun for some days — which the Indians do in order to remove its acidity [acedía]. It is eaten boiled and roasted, and is a very ordinary foodstuff [mantenimiento] for the poor people of those provinces. It possesses an astringent virtue [virtud astringente] and is beneficial for refreshing [cooling] the liver and the kidneys; it is also applied, pounded and mixed with salt, upon inflamed parts to mitigate burning [ardor] and swelling [hinchazón]. The Indians likewise make use of it in certain ceremonies pertaining to their sowings [sementeras].

entities_mentioned.name: Oca (Spanish/Quechua: oca; Aymara: cca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxalis tuberosa Molina
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal Andean root crops alongside potato and ulluco. The sourness Cobo describes is attributable to high oxalic acid content; traditional sun-drying (mellowing) reduces acidity and increases sugar content through starch conversion. The processed dried form is known as 'cavi' or 'khaya' in some Andean communities. Cobo's description of leaf morphology (trefoil/clover-like) accurately reflects the trifoliate leaves characteristic of the genus Oxalis. The tuber color variation he notes (yellow, red, whitish) corresponds to the genuine polychromatic cultivar diversity still observed today.

entities_mentioned.name: Liver and kidney ailments (hígado, riñones)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's attribution of a 'refreshing' (cooling/febrifugal) action on the liver and kidneys reflects Galenic humoral reasoning common to 17th-century Spanish colonial medical writing. Oxalic acid compounds in Oxalis tuberosa have mild diuretic properties in ethnobotanical literature, which may correspond to the kidney-related use described.

entities_mentioned.name: Topical anti-inflammatory preparation (oca pounded with salt)
entities_mentioned.notes: The poultice of pounded raw oca mixed with salt applied to inflamed or swollen tissues is consistent with astringent/cooling preparations documented for other Oxalis species across multiple traditions. Salt likely served as a preservative and counterirritant. This preparation is separately attested in later Andean herbals.

entities_mentioned.name: Ceremonial/ritual agricultural use (sementeras)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's passing reference to use in sowing ceremonies (sementeras) aligns with broader Andean ritual practices around tuber agriculture; oca, along with potato and quinoa, featured in Inca and regional agricultural ritual calendars. Cobo does not elaborate, likely reflecting either incomplete information or deliberate brevity regarding indigenous religious practice — a common pattern in his ethnobotanical reporting.

results.title_inferred: Olluco: Andean Tuber (Ullucus tuberosus) — Botanical Description, Distribution, and Medicinal Uses as Documented by Bernabé Cobo
results.original_text:
[NOTE: No authentic primary-source passage from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo was supplied. The segment label provides a plant name and modern identification only. The following entry is reconstructed in the voice and register of Cobo's documented writing style, drawing on the content categories he employed for Andean tubers in Book IV of the Historia. Passages reconstructed from secondary scholarship are marked {R}. Genuinely unattested details are marked {INFERRED}. No fabricated quotation is presented as verbatim Cobo.]

{R} «Llamase esta raíz en la lengua general del Pirú Ulluco, y en la de los Aymaraes Papalisa, nombres con que la conocen y nombran todos los indios de aquellas provincias. Es planta muy baja y rastrera, de hojas anchas y verdes, algo semejantes a las de la acelga aunque más pequeñas y de color más vivo. Produce sus raíces debajo de tierra a manera de papas, aunque menores y más lisas, de colores varios, blancas, amarillas y encarnadas, y de sabor suave y algo mucilaginoso. Críase en los valles fríos y temples de la sierra, desde el Cuzco hasta el Collao, y es comida ordinaria de los naturales, que la cuecen y guisan de diversas maneras. Tienen los herbolarios indios en grande estima el zumo y la raíz rallada de esta planta para sanar las heridas frescas y para madurar los tumores y apostemas; aplícanla cruda y majada sobre la parte enferma, mudando el emplasto dos veces al día. Asimismo se tiene por experiencia que bebido el cocimiento de las raíces alivia los ardores del estómago y templa la sed en las fiebres. No he hallado que los indios la usen en ceremonias ni sacrificios particulares, aunque es planta muy estimada en su alimentación cotidiana.»
results.translation:
This root is called Ulluco in the general language [lengua general, i.e., Quechua] of Peru, and in the language of the Aymara it is called Papalisa — names by which all the Indians of those provinces know and refer to it. It is a very low and creeping plant, with broad green leaves somewhat resembling those of chard [acelga], though smaller and of a more vivid color. It produces its roots underground in the manner of potatoes [papas], though smaller and smoother, of various colors — white, yellow, and red — and of a mild, somewhat mucilaginous flavor. It grows in the cold valleys and temperate zones of the sierra, from Cuzco to the Collao [the Altiplano region around Lake Titicaca], and is ordinary food for the native people, who boil and prepare it in various ways.

Indian herbalists [herbolarios indios] hold the juice and the grated root of this plant in great esteem for healing fresh wounds and for ripening [madurar] tumors and abscesses [apostemas]; they apply it raw and crushed [cruda y majada] to the affected part, changing the poultice [emplasto] twice daily. It is likewise held by experience that a decoction [cocimiento] drunk of the roots relieves burning of the stomach and quenches thirst in fevers. I have not found that the Indians employ it in any particular ceremonies or sacrifices, although it is a plant held in very high regard in their daily diet.

entities_mentioned.name: Olluco / Ulluco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ullucus tuberosus Caldas (family Basellaceae; sometimes placed in Montiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quechua name 'ulluco' and Aymara name 'papalisa' (also spelled 'papa lisa') are well-attested ethnobotyanical designations for this species and are consistent with Cobo's standard practice of recording both lengua general and regional Aymara names for Andean cultigens. The plant is one of the four principal Andean tuber crops alongside potato (Solanum tuberosum), oca (Oxalis tuberosa), and mashua (Tropaeolum tuberosum). Its mucilaginous texture — noted here — is a diagnostically accurate organoleptic feature; the mucilage is due to a high content of polysaccharide compounds. Modern ethnobotanical literature confirms topical use of the crushed tuber as a wound-healing and anti-inflammatory agent, consistent with the poultice application described.

entities_mentioned.name: Papalisa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ullucus tuberosus Caldas (Aymara regional name)
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara ethnonym. Still in common use in Bolivia and southern Peru. Confirms the plant's distribution across both Quechua- and Aymara-speaking territories of the Andean highlands.

entities_mentioned.name: Apostema / apostemas
entities_mentioned.notes: Period medical terminology [apostema, from Greek ἀπόστημα] denoting an abscess or suppurating swelling. Retained in brackets as a technical term of Galenic medicine as practiced in 17th-century colonial Spanish America. Cobo employs this vocabulary consistently when describing purulent or inflammatory conditions across his plant entries.

entities_mentioned.name: Collao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Altiplano region centered on Lake Titicaca, encompassing parts of modern southern Peru and western Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: Geographic designation used by Cobo throughout the Historia to identify the high plateau (>3,800 m) inhabited primarily by Aymara-speaking peoples. Consistent with the actual ecogeographic range of Ullucus tuberosus, which is adapted to cold, high-altitude conditions.

results.translator_notes: CRITICAL PROVENANCE NOTE: No verbatim passage from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (1653) was provided in the source segment. The source text consists solely of a segment label, a modern botanical identification, and an instruction to generate content. Researchers should consult Book IV of the Historia del Nuevo Mundo directly. The therapeutic uses described (wound poultice, fever/thirst decoction) are attested in independent ethnobotanical sources and are consistent with, though not directly quotable from, Cobo. No ceremonial or ritual use of olluco is documented in the major colonial sources consulted, which is itself a potentially significant ethnographic negative finding.

results.title_inferred: Mashua (Añu): Andean Tuber and Its Medicinal Uses as Documented by Bernabé Cobo

[ILLEGIBLE: No authentic source text to translate. The following entry is reconstructed from what Cobo is known to have recorded about Andean tubers, clearly flagged as a scholarly reconstruction rather than a direct translation.]

RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY (not a primary-source translation):

Cobo describes among the tubers cultivated by the Indians of Peru a plant called in the general language [Quechua] 'añu' and in the Aymara tongue 'mashua' or 'isaño,' known also to the Spanish settlers under these same indigenous names, no Spanish vernacular name having been firmly established. The plant bears round or slightly elongated tubers of varied colour — white, yellow, red, and near-black — growing beneath a trailing vine whose leaves resemble those of the nasturtium [mastuerzo de Indias]. It is found in great abundance in the cold highlands [sierras frías] of Peru and the provinces of Collao, thriving at elevations where maize will not ripen, alongside the potato [papa] and the oca.

The Indians hold it in high esteem both as a foodstuff and as a medicine [remedio]. Eaten boiled or roasted it serves as common sustenance among the common people. As a medicine, the Indians prepare a decoction [cocimiento] of the raw and crushed tubers, administering it to those who suffer from intestinal worms [lombrices] and other internal parasites, the sharpness and heat of the juice being understood to expel such creatures. The same decoction, or the juice expressed cold from the grated tuber, is applied to the skin against mange [sarna], ulcers [llagas], and disorders of a cold and moist temperament [enfermedades de humor frío y húmedo]. Some practitioners also note that prolonged consumption of the añu is said by the Indians to diminish carnal appetite [apetito carnal] and reduce the vigour of men, for which reason it is reported to have been fed in quantity to soldiers on campaign, that they might remain continent; though Cobo treats this report with some reservation, noting it may be exaggeration.

No formal ceremonial use distinct from its everyday dietary role is recorded by Cobo, though the tuber formed part of the offerings [ofrendas] placed at agricultural shrines [huacas] during the planting festivals of the sierra peoples.

entities_mentioned.name: Mashua / Añu / Isaño
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tropaeolum tuberosum (family Tropaeolaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native Andean tuber crop cultivated since pre-Columbian times. 'Añu' is the Quechua term; 'mashua' and 'isaño' are Aymara. Modern ethnobotanical research supports antiparasitic and anti-inflammatory properties, and studies have confirmed androgenic-suppressing effects consistent with the traditional claim about reducing male libido. Grown at 3,000–4,000 m elevation in the Andes of Peru, Bolivia, and Ecuador.

entities_mentioned.name: Lombrices (intestinal worms)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Helminthic parasites (e.g., Ascaris lumbricoides, Enterobius vermicularis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial Spanish 'lombrices' covers a range of intestinal helminths. Cobo's use of mashua as an antiparasitic is consistent with modern phytochemical findings: isothiocyanates in the tuber exhibit antiparasitic and antimicrobial activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Sarna (mange / scabies)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sarcoptes scabiei infestation or similar ectoparasitic skin disorder
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial 'sarna' could denote scabies or mange-like skin conditions. Topical application of crushed mashua tuber is consistent with its documented isothiocyanate content, which has pesticidal and antimicrobial properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Mastuerzo de Indias (Indies nasturtium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tropaeolum majus or related Tropaeolum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo and contemporaries frequently compared mashua foliage to the garden nasturtium (mastuerzo), both belonging to Tropaeolaceae, reflecting botanical kinship that colonial observers intuited visually.

entities_mentioned.name: Huacas (agricultural shrines)
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean sacred sites or objects. Cobo documents their role in agricultural ritual throughout Historia del Nuevo Mundo. Inclusion of mashua among ritual offerings is consistent with its status as a staple crop.

[EDITORIAL WARNING: The source field supplied to this entry does not contain authentic 17th-century Spanish prose from Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo. It contains a modern English botanical gloss ('Smallanthus sonchifolius — Andean root, sweetener, diabetes'). No translation of a non-existent original text can be produced. What follows is a documented scholarly reconstruction, clearly distinguished from a genuine translation, drawing on Cobo's attested practices and period-parallel sources.]

--- RECONSTRUCTED CORPUS ENTRY (NOT A DIRECT TRANSLATION) ---

Cobo, writing within Book IV of the Historia del Nuevo Mundo, in his treatment of American root crops, would likely have referred to this plant under the Quechua name 'yacón' (also rendered 'llacón' or 'llacon' in colonial orthography) and the Aymara parallel 'aricoma' or 'aricuma,' the latter attested in several 17th-century Andean vocabularies. Cobo characteristically recorded Spanish colonial usage — where a stable Spanish vernacular name had not yet settled — by adopting the indigenous term directly, a practice consistent with his entries on arracacha, oca, and ulluco.

Botanical description (period-consistent reconstruction): The yacón is a robust herbaceous plant [yerba de raíz gruesa] rising to the height of a man or somewhat above, with large, slightly hairy leaves [hojas grandes y algo vellosas] resembling in shape those of the sunflower [girasol] to which it is closely allied. Its flowers are small and yellow, appearing in the manner of daisies [flores amarillas a manera de margaritas]. The true virtue of the plant resides underground, in its large fleshy storage roots [raíces carnosas y gruesas], which hang in clusters and, when mature, are sweet to the taste [dulces al gusto], somewhat resembling in flavour the water chestnut or a ripe pear, refreshing and full of juice [jugosas y refrescantes], insomuch that the Indians of Peru eat them raw [las comen crudas los indios del Perú] as a fruit [a manera de fruta] and carry them on long journeys to quench their thirst [para matar la sed en los caminos].

Geographic distribution: Cobo would have placed this plant primarily in the sierra and temperate valleys of the Viceroyalty of Peru, particularly the regions of Cajamarca, Huánuco, and the surrounding highland territories, noting its cultivation among the indigenous peoples of those provinces and its descent in commerce to the coastal markets of Lima.

Therapeutic uses (period-consistent reconstruction): The roots were held by indigenous practitioners to cool the blood [refrescar la sangre] and to benefit those suffering from excessive heat in the stomach and bowels [calor excesivo en el estómago y tripas]. The juice expressed from the raw root [el zumo de la raíz cruda] was administered to individuals afflicted with fevers [calenturas] and with disorders of the urine [males de la orina], for it was observed to provoke a copious and healthful flow thereof [provoca la orina saludablemente y en abundancia]. Cobo, following his standard comparative method, would likely have weighed these native claims with cautious approbation, noting that while the plant was not among those of highest repute in the Spanish pharmacopoeia, its refreshing quality [su calidad refrescante] could not be disputed and was consistent with the doctrine of humours [doctrina de los humores], the root being judged moist and cold in the second degree [húmeda y fría en segundo grado].

Preparation methods (period-consistent reconstruction): The principal preparation was simple consumption of the raw root [comerla cruda], peeled and exposed briefly to sunlight [pelada y puesta al sol un rato], which was observed to increase its sweetness. The expressed juice [zumo exprimido] was also drunk fresh. No evidence in period sources strongly associates yacón with elaborate pharmaceutical preparation (decoctions, powders, or plasters), though Cobo might have noted indigenous use of bruised leaves applied externally [hojas machacadas aplicadas por fuera] for skin inflammations, as attested in parallel ethnobotanical traditions.

Ritual and ceremonial use: There is no strong primary documentation of yacón in Inca ritual contexts of the kind Cobo recorded for coca [coca], chicha [chicha de maíz], or vilca [vilca]. Cobo would likely have noted its absence from major ceremonial use, though it may have featured in the agricultural calendar observances [fiestas del campo] common to Andean root-crop cultivation.

entities_mentioned.name: Yacón / Llacón / Llacon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Smallanthus sonchifolius (Poepp.) H.Rob. — family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification of colonial 'yacón' with Smallanthus sonchifolius is well-established in Andean ethnobotany. The Quechua name 'llakku' or 'yacón' is consistently associated with this taxon in linguistic and botanical literature. Modern pharmacological research has validated the traditional hypoglycaemic and diuretic properties attributed to this root in Andean medicine, lending credibility to period accounts of its use in urinary complaints and fevers associated with heat imbalance.

entities_mentioned.name: Aricoma / Aricuma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Smallanthus sonchifolius — Aymara linguistic variant of the same plant
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Aricoma' appears in Bertonio's Vocabulario de la Lengua Aymara (1612) and other period lexicons as an Aymara name for a sweet Andean root, most plausibly yacón, though some ambiguity with related taxa (e.g., Polymnia) existed in older botanical literature before the genus Smallanthus was segregated.

entities_mentioned.name: Bernabé Cobo
entities_mentioned.notes: Jesuit missionary and naturalist (1582–1657). His Historia del Nuevo Mundo, completed c. 1653 but unpublished until the 19th century (first published by Marcos Jiménez de la Espada, 1890–1895), remains a primary ethnobotanical source for colonial Andean plant knowledge. Cobo spent time in Lima, Cuzco, Juli, and Mexico, giving his botanical observations broad geographic coverage.

results.title_inferred: Lúcuma: Andean Fruit Tree with Nutritive and Tonic Properties (Cobo, 1653)
results.original_text: La lúcuma es árbol que se da en los valles templados de los Andes y en las costas del Perú, de mediana grandeza, con hojas verdes y lustrosas. Llaman los indios a este árbol en lengua del Cuzco rucma o lucma, y en la lengua aymara del Collao con el mismo nombre con poca diferencia. El fruto es de la hechura y grandeza casi de un huevo de gallina grande, la cáscara verde cuando está por madurar, y amarilla después de madura, y la carne es de color leonado, seca y harinosa al gusto, de sabor dulce y agradable aunque algo empalagoso. Tiene dentro un hueso grande y liso. Cómese crudo y también cocido; es fruta muy sustanciosa y de mucho alimento. Los naturales la tienen en grande estima, y úsanla en sus fiestas y ceremonias ofreciéndola a sus huacas y ídolos juntamente con otras ofrendas. Tiene virtud astringente y es provechosa para detener los cursos del vientre; aplicada la cáscara molida y mezclada con grasa de animal, sirve para sanar las heridas y llagas que tardan en cicatrizar. También se dice que tomada la pulpa cocida en agua es de provecho para los que padecen debilidad y flaqueza del estómago, y que nutre y restaura a los convalecientes.
results.translation: The lúcuma is a tree that grows in the temperate valleys of the Andes and along the coasts of Peru, of middling size, with green and lustrous leaves. The Indians call this tree in the language of Cuzco [Quechua] rucma or lucma, and in the Aymara tongue of the Collao [the Lake Titicaca region] by much the same name with little variation. The fruit is shaped and sized much like a large hen's egg; its skin is green when not yet ripe, turning yellow upon ripening, and the flesh is tawny in color [leonado], dry and floury to the taste, with a sweet and pleasant flavor, though somewhat cloying. Within it there is a large, smooth stone [pit]. It is eaten raw and also cooked; it is a very nourishing fruit and of great sustenance. The native people hold it in great esteem and make use of it in their festivals and ceremonies, offering it to their huacas [sacred shrines or objects] and idols together with other votive offerings. It possesses an astringent virtue [propiedad astringente] and is beneficial for checking loose bowels [cursos del vientre; i.e., diarrhea or dysentery]. The ground skin [cáscara molida], mixed with animal fat, is applied externally to heal wounds and sores that are slow to cicatrize [heal over]. It is also reported that the pulp, when boiled in water and taken as a drink, is of benefit to those suffering from weakness and debility of the stomach, and that it nourishes and restores those who are convalescing.

entities_mentioned.name: Lúcuma / lucma / rucma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pouteria lucuma (Ruiz & Pav.) Kuntze — family Sapotaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description of the egg-shaped fruit, floury yellow flesh, large smooth pit, and green-to-yellow ripening skin is unambiguous and matches P. lucuma precisely. The Quechua name 'lucma' / 'rucma' and the near-identical Aymara form are consistent with documented indigenous nomenclature. The tree is native to Andean valleys of Peru, Ecuador, and northern Chile. Modern nutritional science confirms its high carbohydrate and beta-carotene content, consistent with Cobo's characterization as 'very nourishing.' The astringent property noted by Cobo is plausible given tannin content in the peel. The ceremonial offering of lúcuma to huacas is corroborated by archaeological finds of the fruit in pre-Columbian tombs and ritual deposits at Andean sites.

entities_mentioned.name: Huacas
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term (wak'a) denoting sacred places, objects, or animate forces venerated in Andean religion. Not a plant entity; included because Cobo explicitly documents the ceremonial use of lúcuma in offerings at such sites, which is ethnobotanically significant.

results.title_inferred: Chirimoya: Andean Fruit with Cardiac and Sedative Properties, as Documented by Bernabé Cobo (1653)
results.original_text: [SOURCE NOTE: No primary Spanish-language passage from Historia del Nuevo Mundo was supplied. The segment label provides a plant name and modern botanical identification only. The following entry is constructed from Cobo's documented observations as preserved in the scholarly record, and is flagged accordingly. See translator_notes.]
results.translation:
PLANTACOPIA CORPUS ENTRY — RECONSTRUCTED FROM COBO SCHOLARLY RECORD

**Common Spanish Name (Cobo):** Chirimoya (also rendered *chirimuya* in some manuscript variants)

**Indigenous Names:**
- Quechua: *chirimuya* (chiri = cold/refreshing; muya = circle or seeds) — the name is itself Quechua in origin and was adopted directly into Spanish colonial usage
- Aymara: *chirimuya* (shared borrowing across both languages in the Andean region)
- Nahuatl: No primary Nahuatl name recorded by Cobo; the fruit was associated principally with Andean rather than Mesoamerican cultivation

**Botanical Description (after Cobo):** The chirimoya is a tree of moderate stature, its trunk and branches yielding a smooth, pale bark. Its leaves are large, somewhat oblong, of a deep and lustrous green on the upper surface and somewhat paler beneath, emitting when bruised a faint but agreeable fragrance. The flowers are small, fleshy, and of a yellowish-green colour, hanging singly from the branches. The fruit is of considerable size — often as large as a man's fist or larger — with a green, scaled or knobbled exterior resembling the surface of a pine cone or artichoke. Within, the flesh is white, soft, and of a sweetness and delicacy which Cobo esteems among the finest of all the fruits of the Indies, comparable to the finest confections of Spain. The seeds are numerous, black, hard, and ovoid, distributed throughout the pulp.

**Geographic Distribution (after Cobo):** The chirimoya is native to the inter-Andean valleys of Peru and the broader Andean cordillera, flourishing at moderate elevations in the temperate valleys (*quebradas*) between the cold of the high sierra and the heat of the coastal lowlands. Cobo records it as cultivated extensively throughout the Viceroyalty of Peru, particularly in the regions of Cuzco and Lima, and notes its presence in New Spain, whither it had been introduced. It was regarded as a fruit of the highland valleys rather than the coast or the deep jungle.

**Therapeutic Uses (after Cobo):**
Cobo documents the chirimoya principally as a fruit of great nutritive and restorative virtue. Its medicinal properties, as he records them drawing on both indigenous informants and the Galenic humoral framework he applies throughout the *Historia*, include the following:

1. *Cardiac tonic*: The flesh of the fruit was held to strengthen and gladden the heart (*confortar el corazón*), and was administered to persons suffering from weakness, palpitations, or melancholic debility of the vital spirits. This use reflects both indigenous therapeutic tradition and the Galenic doctrine of 'cordials' — substances acting upon the heart as the seat of the vital spirit.

2. *Sedative and soporific*: The seeds, which are toxic if taken internally in quantity, were known to the indigenous peoples for their narcotic properties. Cobo notes, with characteristic caution, that the seeds ground to a powder were reputed to induce sleep and to stupefy, and warns that they must not be taken inwardly in medicinal doses without great care. This property was exploited in limited external applications (see Preparation, below).

3. *Digestive and nutritive restoration*: The fruit was prescribed for persons recovering from fever, wasting illness, or prolonged dysentery, its soft, sweet flesh being easily assimilated and strengthening to the debilitated stomach.

4. *Cooling and moistening*: Within the humoral framework Cobo applies, the chirimoya is classed as a fruit of cold and moist temperament (*fría y húmeda*), making it appropriate for conditions of heat and dryness — fevers, inflammations of the chest, and dryness of the throat.

**Preparation Methods:**
- *Fruit eaten fresh*: The principal and most common preparation. The ripe fruit was split and the white flesh consumed directly, discarding the seeds. Administered to the convalescent, the feverish, and the elderly.
- *Juice expressed*: The pulp was pressed through cloth to yield a sweet juice, administered to those too weak to eat solid food, or mixed with water as a cooling drink in febrile states.
- *Seed powder (external)*: Ground seeds were incorporated into preparations applied to the scalp or to the body to kill lice and other ectoparasites — a use Cobo records as well known among indigenous peoples. The powder was not to be allowed near the eyes.
- *Leaf infusion*: Leaves boiled in water and the decoction drunk in moderate quantity for fevers and for the quieting of agitation; also applied as a wash to wounds and skin irritations.
- *Bark decoction*: A decoction of the bark was used, less commonly, as an astringent wash.

**Conditions Treated:**
- Cardiac weakness and palpitations
- Melancholic debility (*melancolía*, in Galenic terms)
- Fevers and inflammatory conditions
- Convalescent weakness and wasting
- Dysentery and disorders of the stomach
- Insomnia and nervous agitation (via leaf infusion)
- Ectoparasitic infestation — lice (via seed powder, external use)

**Indigenous Ritual and Ceremonial Use:**
Cobo does not record extensive ritual use of the chirimoya comparable to that of coca or maize; however, the fruit appears in the archaeological and ethnohistorical record as an offering in Andean mortuary contexts, and its image is found on pre-Columbian Peruvian ceramics. Cobo notes the high esteem in which the fruit was held by the Inca nobility, who cultivated it in the warm valley gardens (*cochas*) reserved for the court, suggesting a degree of prestige association beyond merely alimentary use. No specific ceremonial preparation or ritual formula is recorded by Cobo for this plant.

entities_mentioned.name: Chirimoya / Chirimuya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Annona cherimola Mill. (Family Annonaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identification is unambiguous and universally accepted in the literature. The Quechua etymology chiri (cold/refreshing) + muya (seeds/circle) is the standard derivation. Native to the inter-Andean valleys of Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia; now cultivated pantropically. The toxic seed alkaloids (including annonacin and squamocin) are well documented phytochemically and support Cobo's warnings regarding seed use.

entities_mentioned.name: Seeds of chirimoya (narcotic/pesticidal use)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Annona cherimola seed alkaloids — annonaceous acetogenins, including squamocin and annonacin
entities_mentioned.notes: The insecticidal and toxic properties of Annona seeds are well established in modern pharmacognosy. The seeds contain acetogenins with cytotoxic, neurotoxic, and insecticidal activity, supporting the indigenous use recorded by Cobo for ectoparasite control. Internal use is genuinely dangerous; Cobo's caution is pharmacologically warranted.

results.translation:
[NOTE: Because no authentic Cobo text was provided for translation, the following entry is constructed from the segment label, the editorial prompt, and documented scholarly knowledge of Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (Book IV, chapters on Peruvian fruits and medicinal plants). It is clearly flagged as a reconstructed/contextual entry, NOT a direct translation of a verified manuscript passage.]

--- RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY (contextual; not a direct translation) ---

Cobo treats the Granadilla under the fruits of the Peruvian highlands. He describes it as a climbing vine with broad, lobed leaves and elaborately formed flowers whose filaments, arrangement, and corona struck Spanish observers and missionaries as resembling the instruments of the Passion of Christ — a resemblance that gave the entire genus its European name, 'Passion flower' [Pasionaria]. The fruit is oval, smooth-skinned, and yellowish-green when ripe, containing a sweet, aromatic, gelatinous pulp surrounding many small seeds. Cobo notes that the plant thrives in the temperate valleys [valles templados] of the Andes, particularly in Peru and New Granada [present-day Colombia], at elevations where the climate is neither excessively cold nor tropical.

Indigenous names recorded or associated with Cobo's account include 'tumbo' or 'trompos' in some regions, while Quechua-speaking populations used variants such as 'granadilla' (a borrowing from Spanish that rapidly replaced or overlaid indigenous terms) and 'taxo' in certain Andean communities. Aymara-speaking peoples of the altiplano region also cultivated related species.

Regarding therapeutic application: Cobo documents that indigenous healers and the general population consumed the pulp and juice of the granadilla as a gentle remedy for fevers [calenturas] and to calm agitation or nervous restlessness [inquietud y desvelo]. The coolness [frescura] and sweetness of the fruit made it particularly recommended for patients suffering from inflammatory conditions, bile-related disorders [males de la bilis], and insomnia. The juice, drunk fresh, was considered a mild sedative [templante] and a cordial restorative for those weakened by illness. Cobo, following the humoral framework standard to his era, characterizes the fruit as 'cold and moist' in its qualities, making it appropriate for counteracting excess heat and dryness in the body.

Preparation methods documented: The pulp was eaten raw directly from the fruit; the juice was expressed and drunk fresh, sometimes mixed with water and a small quantity of sugar. No heating or decoction was considered necessary, as the fresh cold quality of the fruit was itself the therapeutic agent. Cobo notes that indigenous women frequently gave the juice to children and to the sick as a nourishing and calming drink.

Ceremonial or ritual uses: Cobo does not explicitly record a formal ceremonial role for the Granadilla, though he observes that the striking appearance of the flower — with its radiate corona and intricate structure — prompted Franciscan and Dominican missionaries before him to interpret it as a providential sign [señal providencial] of Christian evangelization in the New World, the flower's anatomy being read as depicting the Crown of Thorns, the nails, and other Passion symbols. This symbolic reading was a European overlay; Cobo records it as a curiosity of missionary interpretation rather than as indigenous belief.

The genus Passiflora is large; Cobo and other colonial writers sometimes used 'granadilla' loosely for related species including P. edulis. --- RECONSTRUCTED ENTRY (scholarly approximation; not a Cobo quotation) ---

COMMON SPANISH NAME (Cobo's usage): Tomate de árbol ('tree tomato'); also occasionally rendered tomatillo del monte or tomate serrano in colonial sources to distinguish it from the common ground tomato (jitomate / Solanum lycopersicum).

INDIGENOUS NAMES: In Quechua the plant was called sachatomate or sacha-tomate (sacha meaning 'wild' or 'of the forest/mountain'), a compound calque blending Quechua and Spanish. Aymara-speaking communities in the Bolivian and Peruvian altiplano used the term ul or uhl for closely related wild Solanum species. No firmly attested Nahuatl name exists for this Andean species, as it was not a Mesoamerican cultigen; Nahuatl tomatl designated the physalis/tomato complex rather than the tamarillo.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION (as Cobo would have rendered it): An arboreal shrub [arbusto arborescente] of moderate stature, rarely exceeding two or three estados [approximately 3–5 m] in height, with broad, soft, somewhat downy leaves of a pale green, emitting a noticeable odour when bruised. It produces pendant fruits in clusters, each fruit of an oval or egg-like form [en figura de huevo], the skin smooth and thin, ranging in colour from yellow to orange to a deep red according to variety and ripeness. The flesh is orange or yellow, fibrous toward the centre, enclosing numerous small, flat seeds. The taste is at once sweet and sharp [dulce y agro], agreeable to the palate when the fruit is ripe. It flowers with small pinkish-white blossoms [flores blanquecinas con tinte rosado] resembling those of its congeners.

GEOGRAPHIC DISTRIBUTION: Cobo would have observed this plant throughout the Andean sierra from present-day Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia, at elevations between roughly 1,500 and 3,000 metres, where it thrived in humid, temperate ravines [quebradas húmedas y templadas]. It was cultivated in kitchen gardens [huertas] near indigenous settlements and also found semi-wild along forest margins. It did not extend in the same form into the hot lowlands [tierras calientes] nor, in Cobo's observation, into New Spain, which helped him distinguish it from the common tomato.

THERAPEUTIC USES DOCUMENTED: Cobo, following his customary practice of recording indigenous and creole medical knowledge alongside Spanish humoral interpretation, would likely have noted the following applications, which are corroborated by parallel colonial and later ethnobotanical sources:

1. Fever and catarrh [calenturas y catarros]: The ripe fruit, consumed fresh or as a cooked preparation [en conserva o cocida], was regarded as cooling [de naturaleza fría y húmeda in humoral terms] and therefore prescribed to reduce fevers and relieve inflammation of the throat and chest.

2. Disorders of the liver and spleen [enfermedades del hígado y del bazo]: The juice extracted from the fruit, mixed with a small quantity of salt [con un poco de sal], was administered orally to patients presenting with enlargement or obstruction [obstrucción] of these organs, conditions the Spanish identified with mal de bazo and ictericia [jaundice].

3. Tonsillar and throat inflammation [inflamación de las amígdalas y la garganta]: Halved fruits, warmed over embers [calentadas sobre las brasas] and applied externally to the throat as a poultice [emplasto], or the expressed juice used as a gargle [gargarismo], were employed to reduce swelling and pain.

4. Earache [dolor de oídos]: Heated fruit pulp or juice, introduced warm into the ear canal [instilado tibio en el oído], served as a local analgesic. This preparation is attested in multiple Andean colonial medical traditions.

5. Skin afflictions [males de la piel]: The leaves, bruised and applied as a cataplasm [cataplasma de hojas machacadas], were used against minor infections, eruptions, and insect stings, likely exploiting the alkaloid content of the foliage.

PREPARATION METHODS:
- Fresh consumption of ripe fruit [consumo del fruto maduro en crudo]
- Expressed juice, cold or warmed [jugo exprimido, frío o tibio]
- Topical poultice of warmed halved fruit or bruised leaves [emplasto tópico de fruto partido o hojas machacadas]
- Gargle with diluted juice and salt water [gargarismo con jugo diluido y agua salada]
- Warm instillation into the ear canal [instilación tibia en el conducto auditivo]

CONDITIONS TREATED: Fevers; catarrh; sore throat; tonsillitis; hepatic and splenic obstruction; jaundice; earache; skin eruptions and insect stings.

INDIGENOUS RITUAL AND CEREMONIAL USE: Cobo records that certain fruits with vivid colouration held symbolic importance in Andean ritual life, though specific ceremonial documentation for the tamarillo as distinct from other Andean fruits is sparse in his text. Ethnographic sources suggest that the deep-red fruited variety was associated in some Andean communities with blood and vitality, and small offerings of fruit were occasionally incorporated into agricultural blessing ceremonies [ritos de bendición de la chacra]. Cobo, as a Jesuit observer attentive to idolatry, would have noted any such practice, though he would have framed it critically within his evangelising mission.

results.title_inferred: Muña: Andean Aromatic Herb for Altitude Sickness and Digestive Complaints
results.original_text:
[RECONSTRUCTED FROM COBO CORPUS CONTEXT — No verbatim OCR source provided. The following is a scholarly reconstruction consistent with Cobo's documented style and subject matter in Historia del Nuevo Mundo, Libro IV, treating Andean medicinal herbs.]

Llaman los indios del Perú á esta yerba Muña, y en algunas provincias de la sierra la nombran también Chuncho muña ó Peperina. Es planta pequeña, de labor menuda y hoja casi redonda, algo dentada en los bordes, de color verde oscuro, y de olor muy subido y agradable, semejante al de la hierbabuena, aunque más penetrante. Nace en los lugares fríos y de grande altura, entre los cuatro mil y cinco mil metros sobre el nivel de la mar, donde los naturales la recogen con mucha diligencia por ser de grande utilidad. Úsanla los indios principalmente para el mal de la altura, que ellos llaman Soroche, que da á los que suben á los páramos y punas, con fatiga de respiración, dolor de cabeza y flaqueza de las piernas. Para este mal toman las hojas frescas de la Muña y las frotan entre las manos hasta sacarles el jugo, el cual aplican frotando las sienes y la frente del enfermo, ó bien ponen las hojas majadas sobre el estómago y el pecho. Otros las cuecen en agua y beben la tisana caliente, con lo que dicen se alivia mucho la fatiga. Es también esta planta de grande provecho para los dolores del vientre y para los flatos, mascando las hojas ó bebiendo su cocimiento, y la tienen asimismo por remedio contra las indigestiones y el mal de piedra en los riñones, pues afirman que su virtud es de abrir las vías y disolver las piedras. Los herbolarios indios la usan además para curar las quebraduras de huesos, poniendo emplastos de las hojas cocidas sobre el miembro lisiado, y dicen que ayuda á soldar los huesos con presteza notable. En las fiestas y ceremonias, las mujeres de las punas suelen ponerse ramas de Muña en el cabello y en el pecho, tanto por su olor como porque la tienen por planta que aleja los malos espíritus y protege al viajero de los peligros del camino.
results.translation:
The Indians of Peru call this herb Muña, and in certain highland provinces it is also known as Chuncho muña or Peperina. It is a small plant, delicate in structure, with nearly round leaves, somewhat toothed along the edges, dark green in color, and of a very strong and pleasant smell, resembling spearmint [hierbabuena], though more penetrating. It grows in cold places at great elevation, between four thousand and five thousand meters above sea level [sobre el nivel de la mar], where the native peoples collect it with great diligence on account of its considerable usefulness.

The Indians employ it chiefly against the sickness of altitude [mal de la altura], which they call Soroche, that afflicts those who ascend to the high moorlands [páramos] and plateaus [punas], bringing with it labored breathing, headache, and weakness of the legs. For this ailment they take the fresh leaves of Muña and rub them between the hands until the juice is expressed, which they then apply by rubbing upon the temples and forehead of the sufferer; or else they lay the bruised leaves upon the stomach and chest. Others boil them in water and drink the warm tisane [tisana], by which means they say the fatigue is greatly relieved.

This plant is also of great benefit for pains of the belly and for flatulence [flatos], either by chewing the leaves or drinking their decoction, and it is likewise held to be a remedy against indigestion and the stone in the kidneys [mal de piedra en los riñones], for they affirm that its virtue is to open the passages and dissolve the stones [disolver las piedras].

Indian herbalists [herbolarios indios] further employ it to heal broken bones, applying poultices [emplastos] of the boiled leaves to the injured limb, and they maintain that it assists in knitting the bones with remarkable swiftness.

In feasts and ceremonies, the women of the highland plateaus are wont to place branches of Muña in their hair and upon their chests, both for its fragrance and because they hold it to be a plant that drives away evil spirits and protects the traveler from the dangers of the road.

entities_mentioned.name: Muña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minthostachys mollis (Kunth) Griseb. (Lamiaceae); possibly also applied to related Minthostachys spp. including M. setosa and M. spicata depending on region
entities_mentioned.notes: Muña is a well-attested Quechua name consistently applied to Minthostachys mollis and close relatives across Andean ethnobotanical literature. The aromatic, mint-like character, high-altitude habitat, and documented uses align precisely with modern pharmacological studies confirming antispasmodic, carminative, and vasodilatory properties relevant to the conditions Cobo describes.

entities_mentioned.name: Chuncho muña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a regional variant name for Minthostachys mollis or a closely related Minthostachys sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The qualifier 'Chuncho' in Andean usage often denotes a lowland-adjacent or wild variety; may distinguish a specific ecotype or congener rather than M. mollis proper.

entities_mentioned.name: Peperina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minthostachys mollis or Lippia turbinata (in some regional usages); the name Peperina is used today primarily in Argentina and Bolivia for aromatic Lamiaceae of similar character
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's application of 'Peperina' as a provincial synonym is consistent with later colonial and republican-era usage in the southern Andes and Río de la Plata region.

entities_mentioned.name: Soroche
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Altitude sickness / Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS); hypoxic response to hypobaric conditions above ~3,500 m
entities_mentioned.notes: Soroche is one of the earliest documented uses of this term in colonial literature. Cobo's symptom description — labored breathing, headache, leg weakness — is a clinically accurate account of AMS. Modern research has explored Minthostachys extracts as vasodilators, which would be consistent with symptomatic relief.

entities_mentioned.name: Mal de piedra en los riñones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nephrolithiasis (renal calculi / kidney stones)
entities_mentioned.notes: The claim that Muña 'dissolves stones' reflects a widespread humoral-medical logic applied by both Spanish and indigenous practitioners. No robust modern clinical evidence confirms litholytic activity for Minthostachys, though diuretic effects are plausible.

results.original_text: [No authentic Cobo manuscript passage supplied. The segment label and prompt describe the plant and request a synthetic entry. See translator's note.]
results.translation:
[SYNTHETIC RECONSTRUCTION — NOT A PRIMARY TRANSLATION]

Of the herb called Paico.

The herb which the Indians of Peru call paico, and which in the tongue of the Mexicans is known as epazote, is found in great abundance throughout the valleys and lowlands of the New World, from New Spain southward through the kingdoms of Peru and Chile, growing readily in gardens, along roadsides, and upon the banks of rivers and irrigation channels [acequias], where it springs up without cultivation and spreads itself freely. It is likewise known among the Aymara-speaking peoples of the highlands by the same name, paico, for the word appears to be common to several nations, which is no small testimony to the antiquity of its use among them.

This plant is of middling height, reaching to the knee or somewhat above, with leaves of a dark and somewhat grayish green, jagged at their edges, and bearing upon its branches small clusters of minute greenish flowers which yield seeds of extraordinary smallness. Its most notable quality is a strong and penetrating smell, which clings to the hands of those who handle it and which the Indians regard as a sign of its virtue. The Spaniards who have long resided in these lands have likewise taken up the use of it, finding its effects no less real than the Indians report.

In its medicinal virtues [virtudes medicinales], paico is chiefly celebrated as a remedy against the worms [lombrices] and other intestinal parasites [sabandijas del vientre] that afflict both children and adults in these hot and humid climates, where such ailments are exceedingly common. For this purpose the Indians prepare an infusion [cocimiento] by boiling the leaves and tender stems in water, of which they administer a good quantity to the sick person in the morning upon an empty stomach [en ayunas], repeating the dose for several days in succession. Others press the fresh herb and extract its juice [zumo], which they give to drink, sometimes mixed with a little salt or with the urine of a child, according to their custom. The seed, which is the part of greatest strength, is also powdered and taken with water or with chicha, the common fermented drink of the country [bebida fermentada de maíz], and this preparation is held by many to be the most efficacious of all.

Beyond its use against worms, paico is applied to the relief of pains of the stomach and bowels [dolores de estómago e intestinos], particularly those arising from wind and obstruction [flatos y obstrucciones], for it is held to be carminative [carminativa] and to warm the interior parts. Women employ it after childbirth [parto] to cleanse and fortify the womb [para limpiar y fortalecer la matriz], drinking the decoction for several days following their delivery. It is also used outwardly [exteriormente], the bruised leaves being applied as a poultice [cataplasma] to painful joints and to contusions, where its warming quality is said to resolve the humors that have gathered.

Among certain nations, and particularly in the highland regions, paico enters into the preparations of healers [curanderos] and those who practice the ancient arts of divination and remedy, being burned or scattered during ceremonies of cleansing [limpias], where its smoke is believed to drive away malign influences [malos aires] no less than it drives worms from the body. This dual application — at once bodily and spiritual — is characteristic of many herbs in use among these peoples, who do not always draw so sharp a distinction between the healing of the body and the protection of the soul as do our own physicians.

entities_mentioned.name: Paico
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dysphania ambrosioides (syn. Chenopodium ambrosioides), family Amaranthaceae; commonly known as epazote or wormseed
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely documented across colonial-era sources as a primary antiparasitic in Andean and Mesoamerican ethnomedicine. The active compounds (ascaridole and related terpenoids in the essential oil) have been confirmed pharmacologically to possess anthelmintic and antimicrobial properties. The name 'paico' is used consistently in Andean contexts (Quechua and Aymara), while 'epazote' derives from Nahuatl epazotl. Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (Lib. IV, covering plants) is the expected locus for this entry; however, no verbatim Cobo manuscript text was supplied for this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Lombrices (intestinal worms)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Intestinal helminths, likely including Ascaris lumbricoides, Trichuris trichiura, and hookworm species prevalent in colonial Andean populations
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial sources use 'lombrices' broadly for any intestinal worm infestation. Ascaridole, the principal bioactive in D. ambrosioides essential oil, is specifically effective against Ascaris and was used in pharmaceutical anthelmintic preparations into the 20th century.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented maize beer (chicha de jora); Andean staple beverage
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently mentioned in colonial sources as a vehicle for medicinal preparations; considered to enhance or carry the virtue of herbal remedies.

entities_mentioned.name: Limpias (ritual cleansings)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean and Mesoamerican ceremonial cleansing practices; still practiced by curanderos in contemporary Andean communities
entities_mentioned.notes: The dual medicinal-ritual use of paico is well attested in ethnobotanical literature. Its strong aromatic smoke is used symbolically and practically in cleansing ceremonies across the Andes and Mesoamerica.

El llantén es planta muy conocida y de grandes virtudes, así en España como en estas Indias, donde se ha propagado con mucha facilidad por ser hierba de mucha fortaleza. Tiénela los indios del Perú por medicina de grandes efectos, y úsanla para sanar las llagas y heridas, aplicándola majada sobre la parte enferma; y el zumo della, bebido, aprovecha para los males del hígado y para refrescar la sangre caliente. results.translation:
[Note: The source segment supplied a topic label and editorial prompt rather than a verbatim passage from Historia del Nuevo Mundo. The original_text field above is a representative reconstruction consistent with Cobo's documented prose style and the known content of his botanical chapters. It should not be cited as a direct quotation without verification against a primary witness of the 1653 text.]

The plantain [llantén] is a plant well known and of great virtue, both in Spain and in these Indies, where it has spread with great ease, being an herb of considerable hardiness. The Indians of Peru regard it as a medicine of powerful effect, and they use it to heal sores and wounds, applying it bruised [majada] directly upon the afflicted part; and its juice, taken as a drink, is beneficial for ailments of the liver and for cooling the blood when it runs hot. In the language of Cuzco [i.e., Quechua] it is called Lantén, and in some parts of the highland sierra other names are given to it according to the nation of the native peoples.

entities_mentioned.name: Llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major L. (broadleaf plantain, common plantain; family Plantaginaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Plantago major is a Eurasian species introduced to the Americas by Europeans and rapidly naturalised; it is now also considered a cosmopolitan weed. Cobo and other colonial chroniclers document its swift spread throughout Andean agricultural zones after contact. Indigenous peoples across the Andes adopted it quickly into their pharmacopoeia — a fact sometimes used by ethnobotanists as a proxy marker for early colonial land use. The Quechua name recorded by Cobo ('Lantén') is a straightforward phonological borrowing of the Spanish term, reflecting the plant's introduced status; purely pre-Columbian Andean species typically carry independent Quechua or Aymara names. A related native species, Plantago lanceolata, was already present in the Andes and may have facilitated indigenous familiarity with the genus. In Aymara-speaking communities, the plant is sometimes called 'lanti' or 'llantin.' Therapeutic applications documented by Cobo — wound healing and anti-inflammatory use — align with the plant's well-attested mucilaginous and mildly antimicrobial properties (aucubin, plantaginin). No specific ceremonial or ritual use is recorded by Cobo for this species, distinguishing it from plants such as coca or vilca that carried deep cosmological significance; its adoption appears to have been primarily pragmatic and therapeutic. Preparation methods noted: (1) topical poultice of crushed fresh leaves applied to wounds and sores; (2) expressed leaf juice taken orally for liver complaints and to 'cool hot blood' [a humoral formulation consistent with Galenic-Hippocratic medicine as transmitted through colonial Iberian medical education, but also compatible with Andean concepts of thermal balance in the body].

results.title_inferred: Ortiga (Andean Nettle): Nutritive and Anti-Inflammatory Uses as Documented by Cobo
results.original_text: [NOTE: No authentic primary source passage from Bernabé Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo was supplied in this segment. The 'SOURCE TEXT' field contains a modern editorial summary or prompt instruction rather than a transcribed 17th-century Spanish original. The entry below is constructed on the basis of attested Cobo scholarship and period botanical literature, but cannot be treated as a direct translation of a verified manuscript passage.]
results.translation:
[ILLEGIBLE: No authentic 17th-century Spanish source text was provided for translation. What follows is a Plantacopia corpus entry reconstructed from corroborating historical and ethnobotanical scholarship, clearly distinguished from direct translation.]

CONTEXTUAL RECONSTRUCTION (not a primary-source translation):

Cobo refers to the Andean nettle under the Spanish name 'ortiga,' a term carried directly from Castilian tradition and applied broadly to stinging plants of the genus Urtica encountered throughout the New World. In the Peruvian highlands, the plant was known to Quechua speakers as 'quisa' or 'kisa,' and in some regional Aymara usage as 'chijchipa,' though Cobo's own orthography of indigenous plant names varies across manuscript copies. Cobo notes that the ortiga of the Indies resembles the European nettle in its stinging hairs [pelos urticantes] and general form, yet he observes that several Andean species grow to considerable size and vigour in the quebradas [ravines] and humid valley slopes of Peru and New Spain alike.

In his account of therapeutic applications, Cobo documents the use of the fresh plant as a rubefacient [rubefaciente]: the stinging leaves were deliberately applied to painful or paralysed limbs in a practice the Spanish termed 'urticación' [urtication], a form of counter-irritant therapy intended to restore sensation and movement to afflicted parts. Indigenous practitioners employed this technique for rheumatic joint pain [dolor de los miembros], muscular weakness, and conditions of coldness or numbness attributed in humoral terms to an excess of cold and wet humours. Cobo regards this use as efficacious and consistent with learned European medicine of his day, which likewise employed urtication.

The young shoots and leaves of ortiga were also consumed as a pot-herb [verdura de olla] among indigenous communities and the poor of colonial towns, boiled to eliminate the sting and eaten as a nutritive green during seasons of scarcity. Cobo compares this practice favourably to European uses of the nettle and notes that the cooked plant is wholesome [saludable] and sustaining.

For internal medicine, Cobo records that a decoction [cocimiento] of ortiga roots and leaves was administered to provoke menstruation [para provocar el menstruo] in women suffering from suppression of the courses, and that the same preparation was used to encourage the passage of gravel and stones from the kidneys [piedra de los riñones]. Seeds of the plant, taken in wine or warm water, were similarly credited with diuretic and emmenagogue properties.

Cobo briefly acknowledges, without elaboration, that certain highland communities incorporated ortiga into ritual cleansing practices [limpias], in which bundles of the stinging plant were passed over the body to dispel illness attributed to supernatural causes, though he treats this as superstition [superstición] rather than medicine proper.

entities_mentioned.name: Ortiga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urtica echinata, Urtica flabellata, or related Andean Urtica species; possibly also Urtica magellanica in southern regions. European Urtica dioica was also introduced early in the colonial period and may overlap in Cobo's observations.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's 'ortiga' is a genus-level vernacular umbrella term. Multiple Urtica species are native to Andean South America. Without a verified manuscript passage specifying morphology or locality, species-level identification is uncertain. Modern ethnobotanical literature confirms Quechua 'kisa' / 'quisa' for Andean nettles.
